title: Project Architecture
description: Begin with the architecture document to understand the intended Expo and React Native folder responsibilities and the boundaries originally planned for navigation, screens, components, services, state, storage, and shared types. It also provides useful historical context: several areas began as placeholders, while the implemented application has since grown substantial UI, map-engine, and positioning systems.

title: Application Bootstrap
description: App.tsx turns the project structure into a running Expo application by owning preview navigation state and selecting the home, map, floor, or room screen. Reading it after the architecture overview reveals the current top-level composition and the gesture and safe-area containers shared by every screen.
languageLesson: React function components combine declarative rendering with hook-managed state; a discriminated screen identifier keeps conditional navigation branches type-safe.

title: Screen Journey
description: The four screens form the user journey from discovery to floor selection, room selection, and live map navigation. Together they show how the application passes intent through UI callbacks while each screen remains responsible for one stage of the indoor-navigation experience.

title: Reusable UI System
description: These shared components give the screen journey a consistent visual and interaction vocabulary, including layout scaffolding, headers, search, destination cards, and selection controls. The centralized theme is the most widely reused file in the graph, so it acts as the design foundation for both ordinary screens and the map viewport.
languageLesson: React Native style objects and typed component props let small visual primitives be composed consistently across screens without relying on browser CSS.

title: Map Engine Boundary
description: The map-engine guide defines ownership boundaries among orchestration, rendering, actors, movement, and camera behavior, preventing screen concerns from leaking into engine internals. The public controller then gives MapScreen a compact integration surface instead of requiring it to import every subsystem directly.

title: Runtime Map Data
description: The runtime map JSON supplies the floor geometry, visual and collision layers, assets, spawn information, labels, and route graph consumed by the engine. The controller normalizes that loosely typed data into movement constraints, connecting stored map content to rendering and position validation.
languageLesson: JSON is flexible at storage boundaries, so TypeScript normalization code is essential for validating external shapes before the rest of the application relies on stronger geometric contracts.

title: Interactive Map Composition
description: ArenaMapEngineView composes normalized map rendering, actor overlays, movement estimation, and camera controls into the interactive view hosted by MapScreen. The rendering and camera components separate scene construction from pan, pinch, zoom, and follow behavior, keeping visual layers independent from viewport state.
languageLesson: Gesture-driven React Native views often keep pure coordinate transformations separate from animated rendering state, making zoom and pan mathematics easier to test and reuse.

title: Actor Overlay
description: The actor subsystem converts route-based world positions into sprites positioned over the rendered floor. Its model and layer bridge engine coordinates to screen pixels, while the asset registry isolates bundled image lookup from actor behavior.

title: Movement Pipeline
description: The movement-system document explains the full positioning pipeline from phone sensors through preprocessing, PDR estimates, particle filtering, map constraints, and orchestration. It also clarifies how positioning remains separate from collision extraction, actor display, rendering, and camera control introduced in the preceding map-engine steps.

title: Sensors to Motion
description: Sensor contracts define a discriminated set of raw observations, after which normalization and bounded sample windows produce safe chronological inputs. Step, heading, and combined motion estimators then translate those inputs into displacement and confidence values suitable for probabilistic position updates.
languageLesson: TypeScript discriminated unions let preprocessing narrow each sensor variant safely, while immutable sample windows make time-ordered estimation behavior predictable.

title: Particle Positioning
description: The particle filter turns uncertain motion into a stable pose by creating candidate positions, predicting movement, scoring candidates, resampling, and calculating a weighted estimate. Shared particle contracts keep these stages interoperable, while the filter orchestrator exposes initialization and update cycles to the wider movement engine.

title: Constrained Position Output
description: Map constraints test candidate movement against walkable regions, blocked polygons, and walls so probabilistic estimates remain physically plausible. The indoor-position engine is the capstone: it coordinates normalized sensors, motion estimates, particle updates, and those constraints to produce the next position state consumed by the interactive map.
languageLesson: A barrel module re-exports a subsystem's intended public API, while compile-time contract exercises verify that independently typed controllers, renderers, actors, cameras, and positioning modules still compose.
